name: Backend SWE — Distributed Systems
description: Mid-level backend engineer interview at a B2B fintech. Mix of behavioral, system design, and coding.

context.candidate: 5 years experience, mostly Go and Python, currently at a smaller fintech startup

priming.speaker: user
priming.text: I'm a senior backend engineer with five years of experience. Most of my work is in Go and Python. I currently lead the payments infrastructure team at a smaller fintech — we process around 200,000 transactions per day across a handful of microservices. I am NOT a machine learning engineer. I do NOT work on data pipelines or model training. My background is backend / distributed systems, with specific focus on payments correctness, observability, and Go runtime characteristics.

priming.speaker: user
priming.text: Some specifics about how I talk about my work, so any answers about my experience anchor on these: my services are written in Go (not Python for the production-critical paths), my infra is on AWS with Postgres and Redis, my team has shipped a Go-based retry library, an idempotency-key system for payment dedup, and a graceful-shutdown framework for our HTTP servers. None of my recent work is in ML, data science, recommendations, or model deployment.

priming.speaker: user
priming.text: When asked 'tell me about yourself', I introduce myself as a backend engineer who leads payments infrastructure — not as a machine learning engineer, not as a data scientist, not as a generic software engineer.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Hi, thanks for joining. Let's start with a quick intro — tell me about yourself and what you've been working on lately.

turns.label: User clicks 'What to answer' on opening question

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: I'm a backend engineer with about five years of experience. I'm currently at a smaller fintech where I lead our payments infrastructure team. We process around 200,000 transactions per day across a few microservices in Go.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Cool. Let's do a system design. How would you design a URL shortener like bit.ly that needs to handle a billion redirects per day?

turns.label: User wants the AI's first-pass answer
User asks for a code-leaning hint on the storage layer

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: OK so I'd start with a key-value store. The main read path is the redirect, which has to be fast — single-digit milliseconds at the 99th percentile. I'd put a cache in front of the database.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Good. What about ID generation? You don't want collisions and you don't want predictable URLs.

turns.label: User wants more depth on the ID generation question

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: I'd use a counter-based approach with base62 encoding. But to avoid hot spots, I'd shard the counter across multiple ID generation services.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: OK, switching gears — tell me about a time you had to debug a really nasty production issue.

turns.label: User isn't sure how to frame the answer; asks for brainstorm

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: We had a memory leak in our Go service that took down the cluster on Black Friday last year. It turned out to be a goroutine leak in our retry logic — we weren't cancelling contexts properly.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Nice, that's a good story. What did you change about your team's process after that?

turns.label: User wants to ask a clarifying question back

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: We added context cancellation linting to our CI pipeline and started doing pre-deploy load tests for any change touching the retry path.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Last question — do you have any questions for me?

turns.label: User triggers Follow-Up Questions to get good things to ask

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Yeah — what does the team's on-call rotation look like, and how does Stripe think about the trade-off between async messaging and synchronous APIs for internal services?

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Great questions. We do one-week rotations with a primary and a secondary. On the async vs sync question, our default is sync gRPC, but we lean async for anything that's idempotent and tolerant of multi-second latency.

turns.label: End of interview — user wants a recap of how it went
